shortTitle: Pov: You're Life as EVERY Spill Response Rank

content.text: You join the hazardous spill response team at 22, entry-level, eager, and convinced all spills are just puddles with ambition.
content.imageDescription: A young person, just 22 years old, stands proudly wearing a clean spill response team uniform, clutching a mop and bucket. The backdrop is a lively facility hallway, and their expression is confident and filled with anticipation.

content.text: The first call is a tipped-over mop bucket in a quiet hallway, water diluted with so little soap you debate calling for a biohazard sticker. You mop. You nap. You leave a caution sign like it’s a warning for future civilizations.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of a nearly empty hallway where a mop bucket has spilled a small puddle of soapy water. The protagonist mops up the spill, later seen dozing against the wall with the mop at their side. A bright yellow caution sign is left behind, faced outward dramatically.

content.text: Rank two, paint in the breakroom, nothing hazardous except the color choice. Rank three, an abandoned sandwich in the lab minus its plastic—your supervisor declares it technically organic waste.
content.imageDescription: Inside a drab breakroom, a garish-colored paint puddle is being cleaned up. Next to this, in a cluttered lab, there's a forlorn, half-eaten sandwich with its plastic wrapper missing, while a supervisor gestures at it with mild amusement.

content.text: Rank four is a chemistry student’s failed experiment, foam oozing from a beaker, pink and fizzing like a melted birthday cake.
content.imageDescription: Close-up shot in a laboratory: a beaker overflows with thick, pink, fizzing foam that spreads across the lab bench like a melting cake, with startled students backing away in the background.

content.text: Rank five, the ammonia spill in the boiler room, your mask fogs so you pretend your vision was always this blurry.
content.imageDescription: Dimly lit boiler room. The protagonist, wearing full protective gear and a fogged-up mask, stands over the wet, glistening patch of spilled ammonia on the floor. There's a blurry sense to the scene, mimicking the masked perspective.

content.text: Rank six, fractured mercury thermometer, silver beads on linoleum, everyone suddenly recalls third-grade science and no one volunteers for tongs duty.
content.imageDescription: Focused view of a broken mercury thermometer on a linoleum floor, tiny silver beads scattered. Multiple coworkers stand hesitantly at a distance, no one stepping forward, while the protagonist hesitates with a pair of tongs.

content.text: Rank seven, industrial bleach and acid tango in storage, a chemical romance you break up with three rolls of absorbent pads and misplaced confidence. Rank eight, old pesticides leaking from a barrel, labels faded, the smell hints of apocalypse and last Tuesday’s coleslaw.
content.imageDescription: In a cramped storage room, containers of bleach and acid have spilled and begun to mix on the floor. The protagonist unrolls absorbent pads above the mess with wary determination. In another corner, an old barrel with peeling labels leaks a toxic greenish liquid, with visible fumes and a nose-wrinkling stench.

content.text: Rank nine, reactor coolant, alarms sounding, instructions printed in size eight font from the 1980s; your training didn’t mention how cold panic feels through rubber gloves.
content.imageDescription: Flashing red alarm lights illuminate a high-tech facility. The main figure, gloves gripping a crumpled, tiny-font instruction sheet, stares at a leaking reactor pipe. Their wide eyes are visible through a face shield, sweat and tension evident.

content.text: Rank ten, midnight, a tank rupture, sirens pulsing, street empty except for you, blue lights, half the city’s water sour with something even the pros can’t pronounce. You stand in the spill, suit buzzing, not sure if the tingling means exposure or just adrenaline. It started with mop water. It ends here.
content.imageDescription: Late-night city street deserted under pulsing blue emergency lights. The protagonist stands ankle-deep in a large chemical spill spreading from a ruptured tank, their hazmat suit covered in liquid. Sirens wail, the glow of alarms reflecting off the wet pavement. The mood is tense and surreal.

content.text: Response team, ten levels. You climbed them all, one spill, one joke, one silent, shivering breath at a time. Some hazards are measured in liters. Some in sleepless nights. Both leave a mark. Which rank would you have answered the call for? Drop it below. Stay safe. See you at the next alarm.
content.imageDescription: A sequence panel showing the protagonist thinking back over all ten ranks of spills, from easy to life-threatening, with images of each moment reflected in their eyes. Their face shows resilience and exhaustion. The scene concludes with them standing in front of their response vehicle, glancing back at the viewer, inviting them to join the journey.
